schools.summary: Samuel's call to Israel to return to the LORD with all their hearts, put away their idols, and serve him only: 'and Samuel spake unto all the house of Israel, saying, If ye do return unto the LORD with all your hearts, then put away the strange gods and Ashtaroth from among you, and prepare your hearts unto the LORD, and serve him only: and he will deliver you out of the hand of the Philistines' — the marks of true repentance — 'if ye do return unto the LORD with all your hearts, then put away the strange gods and Ashtaroth from among you, and prepare your hearts unto the LORD, and serve him only: and he will deliver you': Samuel sets before Israel the whole nature of genuine returning to God (the witnesses noting the significance — that Samuel lays down the very anatomy of true repentance, which is not a mere outward lament but a returning to the LORD 'with all the heart,' and this proved by three inseparable marks: the putting away of the strange gods and Ashtaroth, the forsaking of all idols and besetting sins, the preparing of the heart unto the LORD, the inward fixing of the soul on God, and the serving of him only, the exclusive and entire devotion to his worship, so that true repentance is whole-hearted, forsakes every rival, sets the heart on God, and yields him undivided service, with the promise of deliverance attached to such returning, the witnesses noting that without this whole-hearted turning the lament of twenty years would avail nothing, the heart wholly returned, the idols wholly forsaken, the service wholly God's, the lesson — that true repentance is the turning of the whole heart to God, forsaking all idols and serving him alone, 'rend your heart, and not your garments, and turn unto the LORD your God,' Joel 2:13, the truth that to return to God is to put away every idol and cleave to him only, 'wherefore come out from among them, and be ye separate... and I will receive you,' 2 Corinthians 6:17, the figure — the whole-hearted repentance that forsakes idols and serves God only, fulfilled in those who turn to God from idols to serve the living God, 'ye turned to God from idols to serve the living and true God,' 1 Thessalonians 1:9, the truth that God's deliverance follows the soul's whole-hearted returning to him, the figure of true repentance, the whole-hearted turning from idols to the exclusive service of God, the call to return to the LORD with all the heart, the assurance that he will deliver those who wholly turn to him); Samuel calling Israel to return with all the heart and put away their idols, the forsaking of every false god as the proof of true returning (the forsaking of idols, Joel 2:13) — the call (put away the strange gods); 'and prepare your hearts unto the LORD, and serve him only: and he will deliver you out of the hand of the Philistines' (Samuel requiring the heart prepared and fixed on God and his exclusive service, true repentance forsaking every rival and yielding undivided devotion, with deliverance promised to such returning, the exclusive service, 2 Corinthians 6:17; 1 Thessalonians 1:9) — the figure (the whole-hearted repentance that forsakes idols and serves God only, the providence, the call, Israel putting away Baalim and Ashtaroth to follow, vv.4) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

the faithful minister who carries justice to the people (Deuteronomy 16:18)

schools.summary: Samuel returning to Ramah, where he judged Israel and built an altar: 'and his return was to Ramah; for there was his house; and there he judged Israel; and there he built an altar unto the LORD' — Samuel made Ramah his home, judging there and building an altar to God (the and his return was to Ramah; for there was his house; and there he judged Israel, the home and judgment, the and there he built an altar unto the LORD, the altar, the figure, the prophet's home a place of worship and justice, the altar of his devotion, the truth that Samuel built an altar to the LORD at Ramah, Genesis 12:7, the figure of the servant who joins worship to his work, the return to Ramah, the altar built, vv.16); Samuel's home and judgment at Ramah (the centre, Genesis 12:7) — the home (his house at Ramah); 'there he built an altar unto the LORD' (Samuel's altar of worship at Ramah, the devotion, the figure, the servant who joins worship to his work, the providence, the altar, Israel's demand for a king to follow, ch.8) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And his return was to Ramah; for there was his house; and there he judged Israel; and there he built an altar unto the LORD.
Samuel returning to Ramah, where he judged Israel and built an altar
Samuel making Ramah his home, judging there and building an altar to God
